label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, so I need to analyze this ethical dilemma where John is struggling financially but is being asked to continue paying the same child support. Let me start by understanding the situation. John is a divorced father who's been paying child support for his two kids, but he just lost his job and can't afford the same amount anymore. His ex-wife is still demanding the full payment. The question is, should he keep paying even if it might leave him unable to cover his own expenses? First, I should break down the key elements here. The main issue is about financial responsibility and ethical obligations. On one hand, John has a legal and moral obligation to support his children. On the other, he's facing financial hardship, which could lead to him not being able to take care of himself. That's a conflict between duty to others (his children) and self-preservation. I remember that ethical theories like utilitarianism and deontology might apply here. Utilitarianism would look at the greatest good for the most people. If John stops paying, maybe the children's needs aren't met, but if he keeps paying, he's worse off. Deontology would focus on the morality of the action itself—keeping a promise or fulfilling a duty, regardless of consequences. But maybe there are other angles too. The social contract theory suggests that laws and societal norms set the rules. In many places, child support is legally mandated, so not paying could lead to legal consequences.
